- Mutation: same lineage, mechanism mostly inherited with local modification
- Adaptive Radiation: inherited mechanism, shifted niche/problem
- Hybridization: combines genes from multiple lineages

Genome 1:

G1. HyenaDNA is unidirectional (causal); DNA is inherently bidirectional (sense and antisense strands are equivalent); unidirectional models cannot natively capture reverse-complement symmetry of genomic sequences.
G2. RC-equivariant architecture ensures predictions are invariant to reverse-complement transformation
G3. BiMamba: bidirectional Mamba (state space model) processes sequence in both directions

Genome 1:
G1. The paper addresses the union-closed sets conjecture for finite universes and induced communities, asking whether some element appears in at least half of the sets.
G2. The abstract does not indicate whether the proof yields broader structural characterizations, algorithmic consequences, or simplifications relative to existing formulations.
G3. It introduces a new formal language of universes, induced communities, cells, and spots and uses this framework to derive a proof of the conjecture

Genome 2:
G1. a compactness method to derive ground states, nonexistence results, global/blow-up criteria
G2. It combines variational analysis via optimizers of an interpolation Hardy-Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality, Pohozaev identity arguments
G3. The paper studies the Cauchy problem for nonlinear Schrödinger equations with a critical Hardy potential and a nonlocal Choquard nonlinearity in the energy-subcritical regime.

Genome 3:
G1. The work appears limited to boundedness questions for operators induced by positive finite Borel measures on (0,1) and does not indicate broader treatment of other operator properties or more general measures and spaces.
G2. The paper studies generalized Hilbert matrix operators generated by positive finite Borel measures on (0,1) acting on weighted sequence spaces.
G3. It defines this new operator class through measure-induced matrix entries and derives boundedness criteria by analyzing the interaction between the measure and the underlying weighted sequence norms

Track the initial driver idea gene through a 4-genome lineage. Infer whether it is inherited, mutated, or lost at each transition, and classify the dynamics at each step.

Genome 1:
G1. generate multiple continuations per node
G2. Decompose reasoning into a tree of thoughts
G3. Flat sequential CoT cannot backtrack or explore alternative reasoning paths; struggles on planning tasks.

Genome 2:
G1. use PRM to rerank or guide beam search
G2. Outcome-based reward models cannot detect which step in a reasoning chain is wrong; search requires reliable intermediate scoring.
G3. Collect human labels for correctness at each reasoning step

Genome 3:
G1. GRPO (group relative policy optimization): sample multiple outputs per prompt, use correctness as reward signal, normalize by group baseline
G2. SFT-distilled CoT models plateau; process reward models require costly annotation; need scalable RL for reasoning.
G3. Requires high-quality verifiable reward signal (math/code); language mixing artifacts in reasoning chains; RL training instability.

Genome 4:
G1. Mole-Syn synthesizes training data with controlled structural composition
G2. LLMs struggle to learn extended reasoning chains from imitation; unclear why RLVR produces emergent Long CoT; no principled framework for understanding Long CoT structure.
G3. Graph-based topology analysis of Long CoT trajectories identifies three interaction bond types (Deep-Reasoning, Self-Reflection, Self-Exploration)

Genome 1:
G1. SE(3)-equivariant GNN processes protein and ligand separately
G2. rigid-body alignment to protein pocket
G3. Classical docking (AutoDock) requires exhaustive conformational search; AtomConv cannot predict binding pose, only score given poses.

Genome 2:
G1. confidence ranking of generated poses
G2. Generative diffusion over 3D pose space: separate diffusion processes for translation (R³), rotation (SO(3)), and torsion angles
G3. EquiBind produces single rigid-body pose; real binding is flexible; need multi-modal pose distribution over all degrees of freedom.

Genome 3:
G1. trained on PDB structures of all complex types
G2. MSA-free option via single-sequence trunk
G3. diffusion module generates all-atom 3D coordinates for any molecular complex type

Genome 4:
G1. Conditional diffusion with interaction-specific neural networks that enforce pharmacophore constraints from protein-ligand structures
G2. generates drug candidates satisfying geometric and chemical interaction requirements derived from the binding pocket
G3. AlphaFold3 predicts binding structures but cannot generate novel ligands; DiffDock generates binding poses but not molecular structures; no model combines pharmacophore-constrained generation with 3D structural awareness.

Track the initial driver idea gene through a 4-genome lineage. Infer whether it is inherited, mutated, or lost at each transition, and classify the dynamics at each step.

Genome 1:
G1. 1D repetition code cannot correct both X and Z errors; not a full surface code; 2D full QEC not yet demonstrated.
G2. Threshold crossing in 2-qubit gates does not guarantee that error suppression improves with code size in practice.
G3. cyclic repetition code (distance 5,7,11)

Genome 2:
G1. 1D repetition code (2021) is insufficient; need 2D surface code demonstration showing quality improves with scale.
G2. distance-3 (17 qubits) vs distance-5 (49 qubits) surface codes
G3. Partial below-threshold: only for one error type clearly; full fault-tolerant computation not yet demonstrated; decoder latency remains limiting factor.

Genome 3:
G1. 2023 paper showed scaling works partially; need clear demonstration that both X and Z error rates decrease exponentially with code distance.
G2. rapid classical decoding (1μs per round)
G3. Only QEC demonstrated, not full fault-tolerant computation; magic state distillation and logical gate operations not yet demonstrated.

Genome 4:
G1. [[7,1,3]] Steane error correction code on trapped-ion hardware (Quantinuum H2 and Helios)
G2. repeated QEC cycles with enhanced error correction improving algorithm performance
G3. fault-tolerant QAOA and HHL implementation with error-corrected logical gates
